cards.h: Budget depends on the buying type
cards.body: Before you calculate how much money you'll put into a campaign, you need to know how you'll buy it. The buying type is the method by which Meta charges you for ads, and it stems from the difference between buying on an open marketplace or reserving the space in advance. You have two options: Auction, where you compete in real time against other advertisers for the same audience, and Reservation, where you set the space aside at a price quoted ahead of time. This comes from Module 2 and is the foundation of this entire module, because each method uses different math to arrive at the budget. With Auction the cost moves on its own and you estimate it; with Reservation the cost is locked and you calculate it exactly. That's why the first thing a planner decides before talking about money is which of the two to go with.

cards.q: Reservation only supports the Awareness and ___ objectives.
cards.explanation: Awareness and Engagement: the only Reservation objectives.

cards.q: Who wins the auction in Auction?
cards.explanation: The highest total value wins (bid + estimated action rates + ad quality), not the highest bid.

cards.q: With a strict CPA cap optimizing for volume, the bid strategy is ___.
cards.explanation: Cost per Result Goal keeps the target cost per result.

cards.q: Pricing in Auction vs Reservation:
cards.explanation: This defines the budgeting logic of this module: in Auction you estimate, in Reservation you calculate.

cards.h: Daily budget: the average daily spend
cards.body: The daily budget is the average amount you tell Meta to spend per day. The name comes from the fact that you think of spend in terms of a daily pace, not a fixed total amount. You choose it when the campaign runs continuously and has no clear end date, what marketing calls always-on. You define, for example, $300 a day, and Meta manages that spending pace day by day without you having to pause or restart anything. It's the most common option for campaigns you simply want to leave on and keep alive. Note: it's an average, not a fixed figure (we'll cover that in the next card).

cards.h: Lifetime budget: the total amount for the period
cards.body: The lifetime budget is a single amount for the entire campaign period, instead of a daily pace. The name refers to the full "life" of the campaign: you define the total that exists from start to finish. You tell Meta "I have $9,000 for these three weeks" and the system distributes that money across the dates, spending more on the days with the best opportunity. It's useful when you have a fixed budget that can't grow and defined start and end dates. It's also required for two things: ad scheduling or dayparting (scheduling delivery only on certain days and times) and Reservation campaigns. If you need to control when your ads are shown, this is the budget to use.

cards.h: Example: Daily vs Lifetime
cards.body: Imagine two clients. La Esquina Café wants to stay present on social media with no end date: it sets a daily budget of $200 a day and leaves the campaign running month after month. In contrast, FitZona Gym runs a "January sign-up" promotion that only runs from the 1st to the 31st with $15,000 already approved by the owner, and it also wants to advertise only Monday through Friday from 6am to 9am: that calls for a lifetime budget, because there's a fixed amount, dates and dayparting.

cards.h: CBO: the budget lives at the campaign level
cards.body: A campaign in Meta has three levels: campaign, ad set (the ad set, where you define audience, placements and budget) and ad (the creative itself). The underlying question is: at which level do I put the money? With Advantage+ campaign budget —historically known as CBO (Campaign Budget Optimization)— the budget is set at the top, at the campaign level, and Meta distributes it on its own across the ad sets, sending more money to the one delivering the best results. The idea behind it is that the algorithm sees in real time which audience responds best and shifts spend toward it faster than you could by hand. You gain efficiency because the system chases opportunity in real time, but you lose fine control over how much each ad set spends.

cards.h: ABO: the budget lives in each ad set
cards.body: With Ad set budget —historically known as ABO (Ad Set Budget Optimization)— you set an independent budget on each ad set, one level below CBO. Distribution is manual: if you want one ad set to spend more than another, you define it and adjust it by hand. The advantage is that you ensure each audience receives exactly the money you decided, without the system pulling it away to give to another. You gain granular control —ideal when you want to guarantee spend to a specific audience, like a test you don't want to die— but you lose the flexibility of Meta moving money toward where it performs best. In short: CBO = Meta decides the distribution; ABO = you decide the distribution.

cards.h: Example: CBO vs ABO
cards.body: An online clothing store, ModaViva, tests 6 ad sets with different audiences (women 18-24, men 25-34, fashion interests, etc.) and doesn't want to move money by hand: it uses Advantage+ campaign budget (CBO) and lets Meta concentrate spend on the audiences that buy most. In contrast, a dental clinic, Sonríe+, has an ad set for a whitening promotion it wants to guarantee with $100 a day no matter what: there it uses Ad set budget (ABO) to lock that spend by hand so Meta doesn't pull it away.

cards.h: Where it's configured in Ads Manager
cards.body: The budget type is chosen when building the campaign in Ads Manager, the web tool where all campaigns are created and managed. The path is: Ads Manager → Create button → Campaign level, where the Advantage+ campaign budget toggle appears; if you turn it on you're using CBO, and if you leave it off you set the budget lower down, in each ad set (ABO). Once you decide CBO or ABO, at that same point you choose between Daily budget and Lifetime budget from a dropdown. The option to schedule days and times (dayparting) is only enabled if you chose Lifetime, so if it doesn't appear, check that you're not on Daily.

cards.h: Where the budget lives
rows: Budget at the campaign level; Meta distributes it across ad sets based on opportunity
Budget set per ad set; manual control of the distribution
Total for the period; enables ad scheduling and Reservation

cards.label: WHEN TO USE CBO
cards.body: Advantage+ campaign budget is worthwhile when you have several ad sets and want Meta to send spend to the best-performing one. It gives efficiency but less granular control per ad set.

cards.label: COMMON TRAP
cards.body: Daily budget does not guarantee the exact spend each day: Meta can spend up to ~25% more on high-opportunity days and offset on low days. The average is honored over the course of the period.

cards.q: The client wants to show ads only during office hours (ad scheduling). Which budget type is required?
cards.explanation: Ad scheduling (dayparting) requires a lifetime budget. With a daily budget you can't restrict specific delivery hours.

cards.q: The client has 6 ad sets and doesn't want to adjust the distribution by hand. What suits them?
options: ABO with the same daily on each
A low Bid Cap on each ad set
cards.explanation: CBO dynamically distributes spend toward the best-performing ad set, without micromanaging. ABO forces you to distribute and adjust manually.

cards.q: The average spend per day, ideal for continuous campaigns, is the ___ budget.
cards.explanation: Daily budget = average spend per day; ideal for always-on / continuous campaigns.

cards.q: The total amount for the entire period, needed for scheduling and Reservation, is the ___ budget.
cards.explanation: Lifetime budget = total for the period; enables ad scheduling (dayparting) and is required for Reservation.

cards.q: You have 5 ad sets and want Meta to concentrate spend where it performs best, without micromanaging. Which setup?
options: Ad set budget (ABO) on each one
Bid Cap on each ad set
cards.explanation: Advantage+ campaign budget dynamically distributes spend across ad sets toward the best-performing one. ABO forces you to distribute and adjust by hand.

cards.q: What best describes the Ad set budget (ABO)?
options: A campaign-level budget that Meta distributes on its own
A budget set per ad set, with manual distribution
It's the same as daily budget
cards.explanation: ABO = budget set per ad set; the distribution across ad sets is manual (granular control). CBO has Meta distribute it.

cards.q: With a daily budget Meta can spend up to ~___% more on high-opportunity days and offset on low days.
cards.explanation: Daily budget doesn't guarantee exact spend each day: variation of up to ~25% day to day, honoring the period's average.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.h: Small bakery in Monterrey
cards.body: A local bakery wants to advertise only Thursday through Sunday (its strong days) and has a fixed budget of $8,000 MXN for those 4 weeks. It also runs 4 ad sets (by neighborhood) and prefers the system to send spend to the one that sells most, without adjusting by hand.

cards.q: Which budget type do you need to restrict delivery to Thursday-Sunday?
cards.explanation: Ad scheduling (showing only on certain days/times) requires a lifetime budget. With a daily budget you can't restrict hours.

cards.q: To have Meta concentrate spend on the best-selling neighborhood without micromanaging, what do you choose?
options: Ad set budget (ABO) on each neighborhood
An equal daily budget for each ad set
cards.explanation: Advantage+ campaign budget (CBO) dynamically distributes toward the best-performing ad set. ABO would force you to adjust the distribution by hand.

cards.q: With $8,000 MXN fixed for the 4 weeks, does this reinforce using lifetime or daily?
options: Lifetime, because there's a fixed total for the period and scheduling on top
It doesn't matter, the spend is identical
Bid Cap, to not exceed the amount
cards.explanation: A fixed total for the period + the need for ad scheduling = lifetime budget. Lifetime defines the amount for the whole period.

cards.q: Daily budget
cards.a: Average spend per day; continuous / always-on campaigns.

cards.q: Lifetime budget
cards.a: Total amount for the period; needed for ad scheduling and Reservation.

cards.q: Advantage+ campaign budget (CBO)
cards.a: Budget at the campaign level; Meta distributes it across ad sets based on opportunity.

cards.q: Ad set budget (ABO)
cards.a: Fixed budget per ad set; manual distribution (granular control).

cards.q: Does daily budget spend exactly the same each day?
cards.a: No; it can vary (~25%) day to day, honoring the period's average.

cards.q: Which budget does ad scheduling require?
cards.a: Lifetime budget.

cards.h: In Auction you estimate, you don't guarantee
cards.body: In the Auction buying type there's no agreed-upon price: the cost moves based on how much competition there is for the same audience at any given moment, just like the price of a plane ticket goes up when many people want the same flight. That's why here you don't calculate an exact budget, you estimate it. The planner's logic is the reverse of Reservation: you start from how many results you want and how much each result has cost you in the past, and you multiply. The base formula is budget ≈ target results × expected cost per result. The "expected cost per result" comes from your own account's history or, if you have no history, from a benchmark (average reference) for your industry. The number that comes out is a starting point, never a promise.

cards.h: What the cost per result is
cards.body: The "result" is the action you want to happen and that you optimize the campaign for: it can be a lead (a prospect who leaves their information), a purchase, an app install, etc. The cost per result is how much each of those actions costs you on average, and it's calculated by dividing total spend by the number of results obtained. Depending on the type of result, the cost gets a different name, but the concept is the same.

cards.h: CPL and CPA: two names for the same cost
cards.body: 1. CPL (Cost Per Lead): used when the result is a prospect who left their information, typical in lead generation campaigns. 2. CPA (Cost Per Acquisition): used when the result is a closed sale or conversion, typical in ecommerce. Both are simply "cost per result" applied to a specific type of action, so the budget estimation formula doesn't change: only which event you're counting changes.

cards.h: Example: estimating budget with historical CPL
cards.body: An online English school, FluentGo, launches a lead campaign and reviews its history: each trial sign-up (lead) has cost an average of $7 USD. For this month, management wants 200 new sign-ups. The base budget estimate comes from multiplying the target results by the expected cost: 200 × $7 = ~$1,400 USD. That number is the starting point FluentGo presents to the client, making it clear that in Auction the actual cost can rise or fall depending on the month's competition.

cards.h: The budget also feeds the learning
cards.body: The budget not only has to cover the results you want: it has to be enough for Meta's system to learn to deliver your ads well. When you launch an ad set, it enters the learning phase, a period in which the algorithm is still discovering who to show the ad to in order to achieve your result. Think of it like a new delivery driver who doesn't yet know the routes: at first they're slow and make mistakes, until they gain experience. While it's in that phase, performance is unstable and costs jump around. To exit it, the ad set needs to gather enough signal; if the budget is too low, it never gathers it and gets stuck delivering volatile results.

cards.h: The learning phase threshold
cards.body: Meta sets a concrete number: an ad set needs to accumulate roughly 50 optimization events in about 7 days to exit the learning phase. An "optimization event" is each time the result you optimize for occurs (each lead, each purchase); that minimum exists because the algorithm needs enough cases to detect reliable patterns of who to show the ad to. If it doesn't reach those ~50, the ad set enters learning limited, a state where Meta warns it doesn't have enough signal to optimize well. That's why, when estimating the budget, it's not enough to cover the desired results: you must also make sure the spend generates at least that volume of events per week.

cards.h: Example: budget that doesn't reach learning
cards.body: A real estate agency, CasaNueva, launches a lead campaign optimizing for completed forms. The director, afraid of spending, sets a budget so low that it only generates about 20 forms per week. Result: the ad set never reaches the ~50 events in 7 days, gets stuck in learning limited, and the cost per lead jumps around without stabilizing. The correct recommendation is to raise the budget until the ad set gathers at least ~50 events weekly, even if that means running fewer ad sets at the same time.

cards.h: Inputs to estimate budget in Auction
rows: From account history or industry benchmark
Reserve signal/budget so the ad set exits learning

cards.label: LEARNING PHASE
cards.body: An ad set needs ~50 optimization events in ~7 days to exit the learning phase. If the budget is too low it doesn't gather that signal and performance stays unstable. Plan the budget to reach it.

cards.label: COMMON TRAP
cards.body: Treating the estimate as a guarantee. In Auction the actual cost fluctuates with the auction and competition: the estimated number is a starting point, not a commitment.

cards.q: The client needs ~400 leads in the month and the historical CPL is ~$5 USD. What's the base budget estimate?
cards.explanation: 400 leads × $5 CPL ≈ $2,000 USD. It's an estimate, not a guarantee: in Auction the actual cost fluctuates with the auction and competition.

cards.q: A budget so low that the ad set only gathers ~20 events per week. Risk?
options: It doesn't exit the learning phase; unstable results
cards.explanation: Below ~50 events/7 days the ad set doesn't exit the learning phase: the system doesn't optimize well.

cards.q: In Auction the budget estimate = target results × expected cost per ___.
cards.explanation: Budget ≈ target results × expected cost per result (from history or benchmark).

cards.q: An ad set needs ~___ optimization events in ~7 days to exit the learning phase.
cards.explanation: Learning phase threshold: ~50 optimization events in ~7 days per ad set.

cards.q: The proposed budget is so low that the ad set doesn't gather ~50 optimization events per week. What's the main risk?
options: The ad is rejected for policy
The ad set doesn't exit the learning phase and performance is unstable
cards.explanation: Without enough signal the ad set stays in the learning phase (or enters learning limited): the system doesn't optimize well and results are volatile. The budget must allow reaching the threshold.

cards.q: In Auction, the cost per result is...?
options: Variable; estimated with history or benchmark, not guaranteed
Always equal to the Reservation CPM
cards.explanation: In Auction the cost is variable: it's estimated from history or benchmark; it's not a guarantee. The fixed price belongs to Reservation.

cards.q: The expected cost per result is taken from the account's ___ or from an industry benchmark.
cards.explanation: The expected cost per result comes from the account's history or from industry benchmarks.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.h: B2B software firm in Guadalajara
cards.body: A firm selling accounting software to SMBs wants ~300 leads per month via Auction. Its historical account CPL is around $6 USD. The director proposes a small test budget that would barely yield ~30 optimization events per week.

cards.q: What's the base budget estimate for the 300 leads?
cards.explanation: 300 leads × $6 CPL ≈ $1,800 USD. It's an estimate, not a guarantee: in Auction the actual cost fluctuates with the auction.

cards.q: The small budget would yield only ~30 events/week. What do you warn the director?
options: Nothing happens, ROAS goes up anyway
The ad set wouldn't exit the learning phase (threshold ~50/week) and performance would be unstable
The ad will be rejected for policy
cards.explanation: With ~30 events/week it falls below the threshold (~50 in ~7 days): the ad set doesn't exit the learning phase and results are volatile.

cards.q: What adjustment do you recommend to stabilize performance?
options: Lower the budget even more to risk less
Raise the budget to reach ~50 optimization events per week
Switch to Reservation with variable CPM
cards.explanation: The budget must allow reaching the learning phase threshold (~50 events in ~7 days) so the system optimizes stably.

cards.q: Estimate budget in Auction
cards.a: Target results × expected cost per result.

cards.q: Where does the expected cost per result come from?
cards.a: From the account's history or from an industry benchmark.

cards.q: Learning phase threshold
cards.a: ~50 optimization events in ~7 days per ad set.

cards.q: Risk of a very low budget
cards.a: The ad set doesn't exit the learning phase; unstable performance.

cards.q: Is the cost in Auction fixed or variable?
cards.a: Variable; it's estimated, not guaranteed (fluctuates with the auction).

cards.q: Example: 400 leads and $5 USD CPL
cards.a: Base budget ≈ 400 × $5 = ~$2,000 USD.

cards.h: In Reservation the price is locked in
cards.body: The Reservation buying type is the opposite of Auction: here the price is known and set aside in advance, just like when you reserve an event venue at a fixed price months before the party. The cost is expressed in CPM (Cost Per Mille, cost per thousand impressions; "mille" is thousand in Latin) and in Reservation that CPM is fixed: it's already quoted before launch. That's why here you don't estimate, you calculate the budget precisely. Since the price doesn't move, the entire plan is predictable from day zero, which is exactly what you need when a client signs off on a fixed amount and demands delivery guarantees.

cards.h: Reach and frequency: the plan's two inputs
cards.body: To calculate the budget in Reservation you first define two things. 1. Reach is the number of unique people you want to see the ad; each person counts once, no matter how many times they see it. 2. Frequency is how many times, on average, you want each person to see it during the period. With those two figures you get the impressions (each time the ad appears on screen), which is what's actually purchased: impressions = reach × frequency. For example, a person reached with a frequency of 3 generates 3 impressions, so 100,000 people with a frequency of 3 are 300,000 impressions.

cards.h: How you arrive at the budget
cards.body: Once you have the planned impressions, the budget comes straight from the fixed quoted CPM. Since the CPM is the price per thousand impressions, you divide by 1000: budget = planned impressions × CPM ÷ 1000. The classic exam trap is wanting to use the Auction formula (results × cost per result) in Reservation: here you don't estimate conversions, everything starts from impressions. The full chain is: reach × frequency = impressions, and then impressions × CPM ÷ 1000 = budget.

cards.h: Example: planning a launch with Reservation
cards.body: A sports sneaker brand, RunPro, launches a new model and management approved a fixed budget requesting guaranteed reach: it wants to reach 400,000 people with a frequency of 3, and the agency quoted a fixed CPM of $5 USD. The calculation: impressions = 400,000 × 3 = 1,200,000; budget = 1,200,000 × $5 ÷ 1000 = $6,000 USD. Since the CPM is fixed, that number is exact and guaranteed, not an estimate that could jump around as would happen in Auction.

cards.h: Where it's planned in Meta
cards.body: Reservation is built with the reach and frequency tool within Ads Manager. When creating the campaign you choose Reservation as the buying type, define the audience and the period, and the tool shows you a prediction of how many people you can reach and at what CPM. Right there you adjust reach and frequency until the plan fits the budget, and you reserve. Remember that Reservation only supports the Awareness and Engagement objectives; it doesn't work for sales campaigns, which only run through Auction.

cards.h: Planning in Reservation
rows: Impressions per person over the period

cards.label: PLANNING ADVANTAGE
cards.body: Reservation lets you quote the entire plan before spending: ideal when the client signs off on a fixed budget and demands delivery guarantees (launches, dated brand campaigns).

cards.label: COMMON TRAP
cards.body: Using the Auction formula (results × cost) in Reservation. In Reservation you don't estimate conversions: you calculate from impressions (reach × frequency) × CPM.

cards.q: Plan: reach 500,000 people with a frequency of 3 at a fixed CPM of $4 USD. Approximate budget?
cards.explanation: Impressions = 500,000 × 3 = 1,500,000. Budget = 1,500,000 × $4 ÷ 1000 = $6,000 USD. In Reservation this is predictable because the CPM is fixed.

cards.q: In Reservation the CPM is ___ and reach/frequency are planned before launch.
cards.explanation: Reservation = fixed CPM, price known in advance; reach and frequency are planned before launch.

cards.q: Planned impressions in Reservation = reach × ___.
cards.explanation: Impressions = reach (unique people) × frequency (impressions per person).

cards.q: What's the budget formula in Reservation?
cards.explanation: Budget in Reservation = planned impressions × CPM ÷ 1000 (impressions = reach × frequency).

cards.q: Plan: 200,000 people, frequency 2, fixed CPM $5 USD. Approximate budget?
Reservation is worthwhile when the client signs off on a fixed budget and demands delivery ___.
cards.explanation: Reservation lets you quote the entire plan before spending: ideal with a fixed budget and delivery guarantees (launches, dated brand campaigns).

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.h: Brand launch for a regional bottler
cards.body: A bottler in northern Mexico launches a new beverage. Management approved a fixed budget and demands guaranteed reach: 800,000 people with a frequency of 3. The agency quotes a fixed CPM of $5 USD for the campaign period.

cards.q: Which buying type fits a fixed budget + guaranteed reach + fixed CPM?
options: Auction with a low Bid Cap
Auction with Cost per Result Goal
cards.explanation: Guaranteed reach + fixed budget + fixed CPM = Reservation. Auction doesn't guarantee delivery and its cost is variable.

cards.q: How many planned impressions are there and what's the budget?
Why is this budget predictable here and not in Auction?
options: Because the CPM is fixed and reach/frequency are planned before launch
Because the client decides the CPM day by day
cards.explanation: In Reservation the CPM is fixed and reach × frequency is planned before launch, so the budget is exact. In Auction the cost fluctuates with the auction.

cards.q: Calculate budget in Reservation
cards.a: Planned impressions × CPM ÷ 1000 (impressions = reach × frequency).

cards.q: How is the CPM in Reservation?
cards.a: Fixed, known in advance; reach/frequency are planned before launch.

cards.q: Impressions in Reservation
cards.a: Reach (unique people) × frequency (impressions per person).

cards.q: When is Reservation worthwhile?
cards.a: Fixed budget + delivery guarantees: launches, dated brand campaigns.

cards.q: Example: 500,000 people, freq 3, CPM $4 USD
Auction vs Reservation (price)
cards.a: Auction: variable cost, estimated. Reservation: fixed CPM, calculated.

lessons.title: Capstone case · proposing the right budget

cards.label: CAPSTONE CASE
cards.h: Mexican retailer · brand launch
cards.body: A retail client wants a brand launch with guaranteed reach of 1M people and controlled frequency, with a fixed budget approved by management. Afterward it wants a second sales stage with a CPA cap.

cards.q: Which buying type and budget logic do you propose for the brand launch?
options: Auction with Highest Volume and an estimated daily budget
Reservation, calculating budget = planned impressions × CPM ÷ 1000
Auction with a very low Bid Cap to cut costs
Reservation but with the Sales objective
cards.explanation: Guaranteed reach + fixed budget + frequency control = Reservation, with budget derived from reach × frequency × CPM. Auction doesn't guarantee delivery; Reservation doesn't support Sales.

cards.q: If frequency is 4 and reach is 1,000,000 with a fixed CPM of $5 USD, what's the budget?
For the sales stage with a CPA cap, how does the budget proposal change?
options: Auction (Sales), budget = target sales × expected CPA, with Cost per Result Goal
Lifetime budget with no bid strategy
cards.explanation: Sales with a cost ceiling → Auction Sales objective, budget estimate = sales × expected CPA and Cost per Result Goal bid strategy. Reservation doesn't support Sales.

cards.q: Summarize the general rule of how the budget is derived.
options: It's always calculated with a fixed CPM
In Auction you estimate (results × expected cost); in Reservation you calculate (impressions × CPM)
The budget doesn't depend on the buying type
cards.explanation: The budget is derived from the business goal and the buying type: in Auction you estimate (results × expected cost); in Reservation you calculate (impressions × CPM ÷ 1000).

lessons.title: Match the key concepts of Module 3
lessons.instruction: Tap the term and then its definition. Each mistake costs 1 ❤️.
pairs: Average spend per day; continuous campaigns
Total for the period; enables ad scheduling and Reservation
Budget at the campaign level; Meta distributes to the best ad set
Fixed budget per ad set; manual distribution
Target results × expected cost per result
~50 optimization events in ~7 days per ad set
Up to ~25% day to day, honoring the average

lessons.title: Order the budget calculation
lessons.instruction: This is how the planner derives the budget. Reorder with ▲▼ and verify.
items: Define the business goal and the objective
Determine the buying type (Auction or Reservation)
Define target results (Auction) or reach × frequency (Reservation)
Apply the cost: expected cost per result or fixed CPM
Verify the budget reaches the learning phase (if Auction)
lessons.explanation: The buying type defines the formula: Auction estimates (results × cost), Reservation calculates (impressions × CPM). The budget must reach the learning phase in Auction.

cards.q: Ad scheduling (dayparting) requires a ___ budget.
cards.explanation: Only a lifetime budget allows restricting days/times.

cards.q: CBO distributes spend toward the ad set with the best ___.
cards.explanation: Advantage+ campaign budget sends spend to the best-performing ad set.

cards.q: Budget in Auction = target results × expected cost per ___.
cards.explanation: Estimate: results × expected cost per result.

cards.q: Budget in Reservation = planned impressions × CPM ÷ ___.
cards.explanation: The CPM is per thousand impressions, so you divide by 1000.

cards.q: Impressions = reach × ___.
cards.explanation: Reach (unique people) × frequency (impressions per person).

cards.q: An ad set exits the learning phase with ~___ optimization events in ~7 days.
cards.explanation: Threshold ~50 events in ~7 days.

cards.q: Daily budget can vary up to ~___% day to day while honoring the average.
cards.explanation: It doesn't guarantee exact daily spend; it honors the period's average.

cards.q: Which budget does ad scheduling (dayparting) require?
cards.explanation: Ad scheduling requires a lifetime budget.

cards.q: CBO vs ABO:
options: CBO is a campaign-level budget that Meta distributes; ABO is a per-ad-set manual budget
cards.explanation: CBO = campaign level, Meta distributes. ABO = per ad set, manual.

cards.q: Does daily budget spend exactly each day?
options: No, it varies ~25% honoring the average
cards.explanation: Variation of up to ~25% day to day.

cards.q: Budget in Auction:
options: Target results × expected cost per result
cards.explanation: It's estimated: results × expected cost per result.

cards.q: 400 leads, historical CPL $5 USD. Base budget:
cards.explanation: 400 × $5 ≈ $2,000 USD (estimate, not a guarantee).

cards.q: Learning phase threshold:
cards.explanation: ~50 optimization events in ~7 days per ad set.

cards.q: Very low budget in Auction. Risk:
options: It doesn't exit the learning phase; unstable results
cards.explanation: Without enough signal the ad set doesn't learn.

cards.q: Budget in Reservation:
cards.explanation: Impressions (reach × frequency) × CPM ÷ 1000.

cards.q: 500,000 people, freq 3, CPM $4 USD. Budget:
Cost in Auction vs Reservation:
cards.explanation: Auction is estimated; Reservation is calculated with a fixed CPM.

cards.q: Client with 6 ad sets, doesn't want to micromanage the distribution. What suits them?
cards.explanation: CBO dynamically distributes to the best ad set.

cards.q: Sales with a CPA cap. Budget logic:
options: Auction Sales, budget = sales × expected CPA, Cost per Result Goal
cards.explanation: Sales only runs through Auction; with a cost ceiling: Cost per Result Goal.

questions.q: Does ad scheduling require a lifetime budget?
Does Meta or you distribute the CBO budget?
questions.explanation: CBO = Meta distributes to the best ad set. ABO = manual.

questions.q: Does daily budget spend exactly each day?
questions.explanation: It honors the period's average, not the exact day.

questions.q: In Auction is the budget estimated or calculated?
questions.explanation: Results × expected cost; the cost fluctuates.

questions.q: Does the Reservation budget use a fixed CPM?
Does a very low budget affect the learning phase?
questions.explanation: Without enough signal the ad set doesn't learn.

cards.q: Daily budget
cards.a: Average spend per day; continuous campaigns.

cards.q: Lifetime budget
cards.a: Total amount for the period; needed for ad scheduling and Reservation.

cards.q: Advantage+ campaign budget (CBO)
cards.a: Budget at the campaign level; Meta distributes it across ad sets based on opportunity.

cards.q: Ad set budget (ABO)
cards.a: Fixed budget per ad set; manual distribution.

cards.q: Does daily budget spend exactly the same each day?
cards.a: No; it can vary (~25%) day to day, honoring the period's average.

cards.q: Which budget does ad scheduling require?
cards.a: Lifetime budget.

cards.q: Estimate budget in Auction
cards.a: Target results × expected cost per result.

cards.q: Where does the expected cost per result come from?
cards.a: From the account's history or from an industry benchmark.

cards.q: Calculate budget in Reservation
cards.a: Planned impressions × CPM ÷ 1000 (impressions = reach × frequency).

cards.q: Impressions in Reservation
cards.a: Reach (unique people) × frequency (impressions per person).

cards.q: Learning phase threshold
cards.a: ~50 optimization events in ~7 days per ad set.

cards.q: Risk of a very low budget
cards.a: The ad set doesn't exit the learning phase; unstable performance.

cards.q: How is the CPM in Reservation?
cards.a: Fixed, known in advance; reach/frequency are planned before launch.

cards.q: When is Reservation worthwhile?
cards.a: Fixed budget + delivery guarantees: launches, dated brand campaigns.

cards.q: Is the cost in Auction fixed or variable?
cards.a: Variable; it's estimated, not guaranteed (fluctuates with the auction).

cards.q: Example: 400 leads and $5 USD CPL
cards.a: Base budget ≈ 400 × $5 = ~$2,000 USD.

cards.q: Example: 500,000 people, freq 3, CPM $4 USD
General budget rule
cards.a: Auction: estimated (results × expected cost). Reservation: calculated (impressions × CPM).

cards.q: Sales with a CPA cap
cards.a: Auction (Sales), budget = target sales × expected CPA, Cost per Result Goal bid.

cards.q: When CBO over ABO?
cards.a: Several ad sets and you want efficiency without micromanaging the distribution.

cards.q: Is the Auction estimate a guarantee?
cards.a: No; it's a starting point, the actual cost fluctuates with the auction.

cards.q: Daily budget variation
cards.a: Up to ~25% day to day, honoring the period's average.
